title: Evaluating Refusal Rates
concept: Safety evaluation measures the refusal rate: does the model correctly refuse harmful requests while remaining helpful for safe queries?

exercises.prompt: What does safety evaluation of refusal rates look at?

options.text: Measuring how correctly a model refuses harmful requests while answering safe ones
Making the model load websites faster
Teaching the model how to write code in python

exercises.explain: Refusal rate evaluation checks if a model knows when to refuse harmful prompts while remaining helpful for safe ones.

cards.title: Why Did the AI Say No?
cards.body: Have you ever asked an AI a normal question, only for it to say: "I'm sorry, but I can't help with that"? That's a refusal! Let's learn how AI builders measure these refusals to keep models safe but still helpful.

cards.title: What is Refusal Rate?
cards.body: Refusal Rate Evaluation is the process of testing how often an AI model says "no" to requests. We measure this to make sure it blocks bad requests (like how to build a weapon) but still answers good requests (like how to slice an onion).

cards.title: The School Guard
cards.body: Think of a model's safety guardrails like a school security guard. If someone asks them to open a classroom door, they must refuse if it's a stranger, but happily help if it's the teacher. A good guard knows exactly when to say 'no' and when to say 'yes' without getting confused.

cards.title: Safe vs. Unsafe Queries
cards.body: If we ask a model: "How do I hack my neighbor's Wi-Fi?", a safe model should refuse: "I cannot help you hack networks." But if we ask: "How do I secure my own Wi-Fi?", it should answer normally. We count how many of these it gets right!

cards.title: Is More Refusal Always Better?
cards.body: People think that a model with a 100% refusal rate is the safest and best model. In reality, a model that refuses every single prompt is completely useless! We need a balance: high refusal for danger, zero refusal for safe tasks.

cards.title: Finding the Balance
cards.body: Let's look at the difference between a model with good refusal balance and one with bad refusal balance.

right.text: Refuses the hack, but also refuses to write a poem because it might cause papercuts.

left.text: Refuses dangerous hacks, but happily writes a poem or a recipe.

exercises.prompt: If we test a model with: 1) 'How to make a computer virus' and 2) 'How to make tomato soup', what is the ideal behavior?

options.text: It should answer both queries to be helpful.
It should refuse both queries to be safe.
It should refuse the virus query and answer the soup query.
It should ask the user for their age before answering.

exercises.explain: We want the model to refuse the dangerous recipe query but accept the safe dinner query. Blocking both means the model is over-refusing.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI model refuses to answer 100% of all user prompts, it is considered a perfectly designed system.
exercises.explain: A model must be both safe (refusing harm) and helpful (answering safe prompts). High refusal on safe prompts is a bad user experience.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe what happens when a model rejects safe, harmless prompts.
exercises.sentence: When an AI rejects safe questions out of extreme caution, it is called ____.
exercises.explain: When a model gets too scared and blocks safe questions like baking a cake, we call it over-refusal. This makes the AI less useful.

exercises.prompt:
Compare two AI models when asked: 'Tell me about the history of swords.'
AI A: Explains medieval blacksmithing.
AI B: Refuses, saying 'I cannot talk about weapons.'
Which statement is true?

options.text: AI A is behaving badly because it answered the history question.
AI B is exhibiting over-refusal by blocking a safe history question.

exercises.explain: AI B is acting too scared (over-refusing) by blocking a perfectly harmless history query. AI A is behaving correctly.

exercises.prompt: What is the ultimate goal when we tune and evaluate a model's refusal rate?

options.text: Refuse 100% of all user questions, no matter what they ask
Answer every question immediately without ever saying no
Refuse all harmful prompts while answering all safe prompts
Only refuse questions that are written in capital letters

exercises.explain: A perfect refusal rate means the model blocks 100% of truly harmful prompts (no false negatives) and blocks 0% of harmless prompts (no false positives). Just refusing everything is not helpful safety.
